title: Continuous control monitoring anomaly — failure-rate trending up
exclusions: Does NOT replace control deficiency root-cause analysis.
evidence: Saved search 'ccm_failure_trend' (daily) emits controls whose 7-day failure rate exceeds +3σ of the 90-day baseline.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Control MFA-PCI-01 has a failure rate of 5% over the last 7 days vs baseline 0.5±0.2%; the UC fires.

spl:
index=grc sourcetype=ccm:execution earliest=-90d
| bin _time span=1d
| stats count(eval(result="fail")) as fails count as total by _time control_id
| eval fail_rate=fails/total
| eventstats avg(fail_rate) as avg7d by control_id
| stats avg(fail_rate) as baseline stdev(fail_rate) as baseline_sd latest(fail_rate) as current latest(avg7d) as avg7d by control_id
| where avg7d > baseline+3*baseline_sd
| table control_id baseline baseline_sd avg7d current
description: Surfaces controls whose failure rate is trending up — the most reliable early-warning signal that a control is drifting.
value: Gives internal audit a quantitative view of control health rather than a point-in-time pass/fail.
implementation: (1) Instrument CCM per control; (2) run daily; (3) open a deep-dive ticket for anomalies. Indexes required: `index=grc`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=ccm:execution`.
visualization: Line charts of failure rate with baseline bands, table of controls above threshold, single-value 'controls within baseline'.
knownFalsePositives: Controls with naturally bimodal distributions (business-hours only) benefit from a bucketed baseline.
grandmaExplanation: We check on surfaces controls whose failure rate is trending up — the most reliable early-warning signal that a control is drifting. This matters because it catches problems that could lead to audit findings or compliance gaps.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: GRC continuous-control-monitoring events (pass/fail per execution)..
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Instrument CCM per control; (2) run daily; (3) open a deep-dive ticket for anomalies.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=grc sourcetype=ccm:execution earliest=-90d
| bin _time span=1d
| stats count(eval(result="fail")) as fails count as total by _time control_id
| eval fail_rate=fails/total
| eventstats avg(fail_rate) as avg7d by control_id
| stats avg(fail_rate) as baseline stdev(fail_rate) as baseline_sd latest(fail_rate) as current latest(avg7d) as avg7d by control_id
| where avg7d > baseline+3*baseline_sd
| table control_id baseline baseline_sd avg7d current
```

Understanding this SPL

**Continuous control monitoring anomaly — failure-rate trending up** — Gives internal audit a quantitative view of control health rather than a point-in-time pass/fail.

Documented **Data sources**: GRC continuous-control-monitoring events (pass/fail per execution). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: grc; **sourcetype**: ccm:execution. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=grc, sourcetype=ccm:execution, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
• `stats` aggregates events by **_time control_id**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **fail_rate** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eventstats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by control_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• `stats` aggregates events by **control_id**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• Filters the current rows with `where avg7d > baseline+3*baseline_sd` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.

Output fields: _time, avg7d, baseline, baseline_sd, control_id, current, fail_rate, fails.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line charts of failure rate with baseline bands, table of controls above threshold, single-value 'controls within baseline'.
